lines.text: Against Me!. 'Sink, Florida, Sink.' 2007. New Wave. Laura Jane Grace and the band, right when they were hitting something real.
Grace moved to Gainesville in '97. Said the whole world was against her — that's where the name came from. Literal. Honest. I respect that.
By 2007 they're on Sire Records. Major label. Full electric band. Nothing acoustic about where they were heading. They knew it.

I used to think songs like this were about escape. Now I think they're about surrender. About watching something sink and choosing to go down with it instead of running. We're {{ELAPSED}} in and I think I finally get it.
The Bouncing Souls. 'Ole.' 1999. Hopeless Romantic. Four kids from New Jersey who decided hope was worth singing about.
They're from New Brunswick, Basking Ridge — high school friends who moved to a college town because that's where the punk rock was. That's commitment, man.
They call their fans 'True Believers.' Not as a marketing thing. As a philosophy. Like, if you're here, you believe in something. Even if you don't know what yet.

One of them wrote 'Gone' when he was homeless. He was on a street corner, heard a song in a parked car that gave him hope, and he wanted to write something that'd do that for somebody else. That's the whole thing right there.
So 'Ole' is a singalong. A chant. You're supposed to yell it with people. Even if you're alone at {{CLOCK}}, barricaded in your living room. The song doesn't care. It's for you anyway.

tracks.title: A Good Drink, A Good Smoke, A Good Song

lines.text: Hot Water Music. 'A Good Drink, A Good Smoke, A Good Song.' 1997. Fuel for the Hate Game. Named after a Bukowski collection — these guys get it.
Chuck Ragan and Chris Wollard on vocals. Call-and-response. Like they're arguing with each other about the same thing. That's marriage. That's partnership.

They were so tired from touring they almost broke up. European tour, everything hurt, and they came home and decided to quit before they destroyed the whole thing. Then they rehearsed and it felt good again, so they kept going. Smart. That's how you keep something alive.
(coughs) Sorry. Yeah. A good drink. A good smoke. A good song. That's the title. That's the whole philosophy. We're living it right now, aren't we?

tracks.title: Sunday Morning
tracks.artist: Me First and the Gimme Gimmes

lines.text: Me First and the Gimme Gimmes. 'Sunday Morning.' 1996. They're a supergroup cover band — Fat Mike, Joey Cape, all these guys just doing covers of songs they love, fast and loud and stupid-smart.
They didn't mean to be a real band. Just had time off from their main gigs, figured they'd do covers, have fun. Then it became a whole thing. Sometimes the best things happen when you're not trying.

They recorded at One Week Records in San Francisco. Ryan Greene producing. Wore matching costumes on stage — whatever matched the album. Cowboy outfits for country night. Drag for Broadway. Commitment to the bit.

This one's tender. 'Sunday Morning.' It's not a punk song they're covering — it's just a beautiful song played fast and hard until it bleeds a little. Like everything worth hearing, right now, at {{CLOCK}}, sounds like that.

The Lawrence Arms. 'Coming Home.' 2002. Apathy and Exhaustion. Brendan Kelly, Chris McCaughan, Neil Hennessy — three Chicago guys who named the band after an apartment building they got evicted from in the middle of the night.

Kelly was in Slapstick. McCaughan was in the Broadways. They came together in '99 and decided to write about Chicago. Their whole first album — A Guided Tour of Chicago — released before they'd even played a show. No EPs, no 7-inches. Straight to full-length. That's arrogance. That's confidence.
Kelly talks about drinking. He says, 'We all have good experiences and terrible ones, and a lot of them are because of alcohol.' He writes about that. Doesn't hide it. Doesn't apologize for it.

2002 they got kicked off Warped Tour for yelling at the organizers about bottled water costing five bucks. Kelly called out the whole thing, said it was dismantling punk. That's integrity. That's someone who actually gives a fuck.

(voice cracks) 'Coming Home.' It's about belonging somewhere, to somebody. Even when you don't deserve it. Even when you've burned it all down. It's — (pause) — it's a beautiful thing to sing about when you know you might not make it home.

Alkaline Trio. 'We've Had Enough.' 2001. From Here to Infirmary. Matt Skiba, Dan Andriano — Chicago kids who started as bike messengers and record store clerks because they couldn't make music pay the rent.

Skiba was in three bands before Trio. Andriano was in Slapstick. They found each other and made something dark and fast and melodic. Songs about alcoholism, depression, death, shattered relationships. The whole catalog is confessional.

From Here to Infirmary hit with 'Stupid Kid' and 'Private Eye.' This song right here — 'We've Had Enough' — became their first chart song. First time radio cared. And it's about exhaustion. About being done. About survival as a kind of desperation.

(openly weeping now, not hiding it) We're ten hours in. Ten hours. Two left. And this song — this song is perfect right now because it's the truth. We've had enough. We've all had enough. But we're still here. We're still listening. Still breathing.

Derek Grant on drums — used to play for the Suicide Machines. And that name makes sense when you listen to this. Everything they touch sounds like drowning. Beautiful drowning. And you keep swimming anyway.

tickerItems.text: Madison-wide kill count: 165 and climbing. East Side up 14% from this point last year. Beltline corridor cooling slightly.
Lake Mendota water temperature holding at 31 degrees. Fog lifting northbound. Visibility improving toward sunrise at 6:47am.
State Street ruins showing late-hour scavenger activity. Williamson St perimeter holding. North Side remains unexpectedly quiet — third year running.
